title: The EU in an Era of Weaponized Interdependence
gist: A Carnegie working paper maps the EU's import dependencies along three layers (vulnerability/substitution, geoeconomic concentration, geopolitical alignment) and concludes the EU is broadly resilient (81% of 2024 imports low-risk) but acutely exposed in a narrow set of system-enabling inputs. The sharpest warning: the gravest threat is not today's recognized high-risk goods but quietly consolidating 'emerging-risk' products ($341B, 12.2% of imports) where supplier concentration is deepening before policy reacts. The EU faces an energy-technology double bind: rising energy dependence on the US and entrenched tech dependence on China.

themes.theme: Emerging-risk products as the real blind spot

insights.claim: The EU's gravest supply-chain vulnerability lies not in goods already labeled high-risk but in 'emerging-risk' products whose supplier concentration is deepening quietly while political risk stays latent, risking hardening into future coercion tools before policy responds.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Watch quietly-consolidating supply chains (network gear, electric motors, data-processing machines) as forward indicators of the next coercion chokepoints, not the already-mitigated headline items.
insights.quote: the gravest vulnerabilities may stem not from goods already recognized as high risk but from those whose risk profiles are still taking shape.
insights.locator: Emerging-Risk Products / 'The most important insight of this section'

insights.claim: EU emerging-risk imports totaled roughly $341 billion in 2024, or 12.2 percent of overall imports, led by network communication equipment, electric motors, and data-processing machines (together $76.4 billion), plus aircraft parts and printing/engineering machines.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: These categories sit one shock away from becoming strategic liabilities; suppliers gaining share here are positioned for future pricing/leverage power.
insights.locator: Emerging-Risk Products / 'In 2024, the EU imported 267 emerging-risk products'

insights.claim: Emerging-risk imports skew toward electronics (25.1%) and machinery rather than minerals, signaling the EU's future dependence frontier is shifting from primary commodities to technology-intensive and capital-goods sectors where China's role as external supplier is most pronounced.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: China's industrial upgrading converts the EU's dependence vector toward higher-value tech goods—a structural tailwind for Chinese advanced-manufacturing exporters into Europe.
insights.quote: the EU's dependence frontier is shifting from primary commodities toward manufactured and technology-intensive goods, a trend that aligns with broader patterns of Chinese industrial upgrading
insights.locator: Emerging-Risk Products / 'The overall picture suggests'

insights.claim: EU supplier concentration is self-reinforcing because external suppliers' shares of emerging-risk imports closely mirror their shares of high-dependence imports, meaning today's emerging risks will become tomorrow's entrenched dependencies absent active diversification.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Path-dependence means incumbents (esp. China) entrench unless Brussels intervenes early—diversification policy timing is the key variable to track.
insights.quote: Without an active policy of diversification, today's emerging risks are likely to become tomorrow's entrenched dependencies.
insights.locator: Emerging-Risk Products / 'The fact that external suppliers' shares'

insights.claim: The EU faces an 'energy-technology double bind': increasing energy dependence on the United States (crude petroleum and LNG, post-Russia-Ukraine) alongside an entrenched technology dependence on China.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: EU strategic autonomy is squeezed from two sides—US LNG/oil exporters and Chinese tech suppliers both gain structural leverage over Europe.
insights.quote: the EU faces an energy-technology double bind: an increasing energy dependence on the United States alongside a technology dependence on China.
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains in the Age of Geopolitical Competition / 'In this context'

insights.claim: The US is the EU's most paradoxical partner: the largest supply-chain anchor in the strategic core, yet declining political convergence and unpredictable US trade policy are turning the EU's primary security partner into one of its largest systemic supply-chain risk multipliers.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: US reliability as a supplier is no longer a constant—transatlantic energy/minerals dependence now carries political-risk premium that was previously ignored.
insights.quote: transforming the EU's primary security partner into one of its largest systemic supply-chain risk multipliers.
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains / 'By contrast, the United States is the EU's most paradoxical partner'

insights.claim: Crude petroleum has by far the largest single-product GDP footprint among EU high-dependence imports at ~1.45 percent of GDP, despite only a moderate composite vulnerability-substitution score of ~0.21; LNG and natural gas add ~0.35% and ~0.25% with higher composite scores.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Energy remains the dominant macro exposure by economic weight even if substitutable—oil/gas supply shocks still carry the biggest GDP transmission for the EU.
insights.locator: The Economic Value of High-Dependence Imports / 'crude petroleum stands out'

insights.claim: Lithium-ion batteries are the single largest EU high-risk import at $47.6 billion, nearly 37 percent of all high-risk imports in 2024, having migrated into the high-vulnerability/hard-to-substitute quadrant since 2019.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Battery supply is the EU's most concentrated newly-acute risk—central to EV and grid-storage transitions and thus to green-transition investment theses.
insights.locator: High-Risk Products / 'lithium-ion batteries emerge as the most significant'

insights.claim: Despite EU battery-manufacturing expansion via Northvolt and ACC plus Asian investments in Hungary and Germany, the EU remains dual-dependent on Chinese intellectual property, critical components, and finished cells, so domestic capacity cannot guarantee technological sovereignty or supply-chain resilience.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: European battery 'champions' do not equal independence—Chinese IP/component leverage persists even with local cell production, a caution on EU battery-localization bets.
insights.quote: the battery supply chain still relies on Chinese intellectual property, critical components, and finished cells.
insights.locator: High-Risk Products / 'Despite Europe's rapid expansion'

insights.claim: China supplies 52.8% of EU lithium-ion batteries, 54.5% of computers, 40.7% of smartphones, and 63.7% of photovoltaic cells, while intra-EU trade covers 38.8%, 35%, 32.4%, and 27.7% respectively—though some 'intra-EU' supply is Chinese imports re-exported or made by Chinese-affiliated firms inside the EU.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Headline intra-EU shares overstate genuine European capacity; true China exposure in electronics/solar is higher than nominal trade stats imply.
insights.locator: High-Dependence Imports / 'while China accounts for 52.8 percent'

themes.theme: Concentration as the core risk geometry

insights.claim: The EU's supply-chain risk is structurally intensive not extensive: of ~EUR 2.45 trillion ($2.85T) of 2024 imports, 81 percent were low-risk, with vulnerability concentrated in system-enabling inputs—energy, electronics, advanced components, and critical minerals.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Reframes EU risk away from broad decoupling toward 'selective de-risking' of a narrow input set—policy and capital should target chokepoints, not aggregate trade volume.
insights.quote: the EU's exposure is not extensive but structurally intensive.
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains / 'Of an estimated total import value'

insights.claim: In 2024 five countries accounted for 16% of EU critical-supply imports, the top ten for 28.6%, the top twenty for 46.4%, and the top thirty for 60.3%, with the 50% threshold crossed at only ~20-25 countries—roughly one-sixth of trading partners.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Critical-supply influence is steeply concentrated, so disruption from a handful of suppliers can cascade—monitor the top ~20-30 partners for coercion capability.
insights.locator: The Capability Nexus / 'In 2024, five countries accounted for'

insights.claim: Minerals are the most strategically critical sector, representing ~2.3 percent of EU GDP (the largest of any sector) with an IVI-SDI score of ~0.22, combining limited supplier diversity with substantial economic weight; electronics, agriculture, and machinery follow.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Critical minerals carry the heaviest combined economic-and-vulnerability load—prime target for stockpiling, offtake, and allied co-production policy.
insights.locator: The Economic Value of High-Dependence Imports / 'the minerals sector emerges as especially critical'

insights.claim: High-vulnerability, hard-to-substitute products rose from 6.8% of EU imports in 2019 to 7.5% in 2024, driven partly by the growing importance of lithium-ion batteries, while ~89% of imports stayed in low-vulnerability quadrants.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: The most-critical import share is creeping up slowly but measurably—batteries are the marginal driver of worsening structural exposure.
insights.locator: Economic Analysis / 'By 2024, the percentage of EU imports'

insights.claim: Using UN General Assembly voting (2020-2025) as a proxy for geopolitical affinity crossed with supply-chain importance, Carnegie sorts EU partners into four groups; the 'zone of dependence'—10 countries including China, Brazil, and India with high criticality but weak EU affinity—holds the most dangerous dependencies (EUR 462B / $540B, 37% of critical supplies, China alone 25%).
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: The China/Brazil/India dependence cluster is where economic leverage most readily converts to coercion—the priority watchlist for de-risking and political-risk pricing.
insights.quote: The most dangerous supply dependencies are those at the intersection of high substitution difficulty, high forward-linkage intensity, and low political convergence
insights.locator: The Intent Nexus / 'The zone of dependence includes ten countries'

insights.claim: The 'strategic core' of 18 reliable, structurally capable EU partners—including the US, Norway, and UK—accounted for EUR 485B ($567B), 39% of critical supplies in 2024, with the US alone at 9.6%.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Even the 'safe' core is concentrated in a few allies; reliability there is now contingent on US political drift, eroding the diversification it appears to provide.
insights.locator: The Intent Nexus / 'The strategic core includes eighteen countries'

insights.claim: By import value of high-dependence products, China leads non-EU suppliers at 15.6%, ahead of the US at 6.2%, Norway 4.2%, and UK 2.5%—a ranking that inverts the product-count ranking where the UK, US, and China are nearly tied (~1,850-1,870 products each).
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Value-weighted exposure, not breadth, is what matters—China's concentrated high-value position is the real dependency despite the UK leading on product count.
insights.locator: Key Suppliers of High-Dependence Imports / 'Among the non-EU countries'

insights.claim: US-EU supply linkages concentrate in capital-intensive, regulation-heavy sectors (inorganics, vehicles where the US is the sole significant external partner; minerals 20.3% of high-risk), whereas China's penetration is strongest in labor-intensive manufacturing and consumer technology (electronics 22.6% of imports, over triple the US share).
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: The two rivals dominate different EU import segments—China in consumer tech/electronics, the US in vehicles/minerals—so de-risking strategy must be sector-specific.
insights.locator: Geoeconomic Analysis / 'This suggests that U.S. supply-chain linkages'

insights.claim: Ülgen argues the correct EU response is 'selective de-risking'—targeting only inputs whose disruption cascades across the economy with a discriminating mix of diversification, stockpiling, industrial support, and economic diplomacy—rather than a generalized, economically unrealistic reduction of external dependence.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Signals EU policy direction away from broad decoupling toward chokepoint-focused intervention—relevant for firms in targeted critical-input sectors expecting subsidies/stockpiling demand.
insights.quote: The real task is selective de-risking: identifying those inputs whose disruption would have cascading effects across the European economy
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains / 'Because the union's vulnerabilities are clustered'

insights.claim: The January 2026 EU-India free-trade agreement is framed as an instrumental step to secure that partnership, while the lack of progress modernizing the EU-Turkey Customs Union is criticized given Turkey ranks as the sixth-most-critical EU trading partner for economic security, behind the US, China, and the UK.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Trade-policy moves toward India and (stalled) Turkey are diversification signals; Turkey's overlooked criticality is a watch item for EU economic-security policy.
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains / 'the union's January 2026 conclusion'

insights.claim: In a multipolar order economic dependence often precedes geopolitical leverage, so resilience is not self-sufficiency but disciplined management of asymmetric interdependence—reducing exposure where criticality and political divergence overlap while preserving upstream technological advantages.
insights.speaker: Sinan Ülgen
insights.so_what: Defines the strategic-autonomy doctrine guiding EU industrial/trade policy: targeted, not autarkic—shapes where subsidies and restrictions will land.
insights.quote: Strategic autonomy, in this sense, is neither decoupling nor alignment but disciplined governance of the power embedded in supply chains.
insights.locator: Critical Supply Chains / 'In the emerging multipolar order'
